does: This paper proposes warmPref-PS, a posterior sampling algorithm for finite-armed linear bandits that leverages offline preference data to warm-start online learning. The core contribution is modeling expert competence—the reliability of preference annotations—to effectively incorporate noisy offline rankings into an online bandit setting. The algorithm combines offline preference feedback with online reward signals, allowing practitioners to use human preference datasets (common in RLHF) as initialization for adaptive exploration rather than treating online and offline data as separate pipelines. The paper provides theoretical analysis of Bayesian regret bounds that characterize how the quality of the offline dataset affects online learning efficiency.
connects: This work bridges a gap between practical RLHF systems and bandit learning theory. Traditional online bandit algorithms assume real-valued reward signals, while RLHF practitioners typically collect preference comparisons which are noisier but more reliable than direct scores. Prior work on contextual bandits and offline RL has explored leveraging historical data, but these approaches don't naturally handle preference feedback or unknown expert competence. The paper extends posterior sampling—a classical Thompson sampling approach—to the preference-feedback regime and incorporates expert modeling similar to learning-to-rank literature. It responds to the practical bottleneck in RLHF where online human feedback is expensive, proposing a principled way to exploit accumulated offline preferences for improved active data collection.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more sample-efficient RLHF by systematically combining existing preference datasets with targeted online collection. The competence modeling component could generalize beyond bandits to richer contextual settings and larger action spaces, potentially improving fine-tuning of large language and image models. The work enables future research on multi-annotator preference aggregation, where different offline sources have varying reliability. It also suggests applications to adaptive curriculum learning for generative models, where an agent learns which preference queries are most informative. The theoretical framework for handling preference feedback in online learning could extend to more complex reward structures and non-linear models relevant to modern deep RL.
